title: The 10x Reality: How AI is Redefining Software Engineering
author: Eliran Elnasi
text:
The 10x Reality: How AI is Redefining Software Engineering
The role isn’t disappearing. It’s evolving from writing code to designing systems.
The 3-Hour Fix
Two weeks ago, I fixed a performance bug that would have taken me a full day (at least) just two years ago.
A user reported that typing felt “laggy” in a text input. No stack trace. No clear reproduction steps.
In the old world, I would have:
- Spent an hour trying to reproduce it.
- Another hour adding
console.logs
everywhere. - Half a day reading through component hierarchies.
- More time second-guessing my fix approach.
Instead, I had a conversation. Three hours later: root cause identified, fix implemented, tests added, 60% performance improvement measured, PR merged.
That’s not automation. That’s amplification.
Defining the “10x Reality”
For years, the “10x Engineer” was a myth — a unicorn that recruiters chased and developers rolled their eyes at. But in 2026, the 10x Reality is actually here.
When I say developers are entering this reality, I don’t mean we were born geniuses. I mean the friction has evaporated.
The Myth: One developer working 10x harder than everyone else.
The Reality: Every developer having 10x the reach because the friction of “figuring out how” has been removed.
Before AI:
- Bottleneck: Knowledge access.
- Workflow: You either knew the answer or you Googled for 30 minutes.
- Friction: Context-switching between “thinking” and “searching” killed flow.
- Collaboration: Solo debugging meant talking to yourself (or a rubber duck).
After AI:
- Bottleneck: Asking the right questions.
- Workflow: Knowledge is on-demand.
- Friction: You stay in flow because the thinking partner is instant.
- Collaboration: “Rubber duck debugging” actually talks back.
The “10x Reality” isn’t about typing faster. It’s about thinking faster.
The New Developer Skill Stack
The skills that made a great developer in 2020 aren’t the same ones that matter in 2026.
📉 Declining in Value:
- Memorizing syntax: The AI knows every API better than you do.
- Boilerplate generation: Autocomplete handles the repetition.
- Stack Overflow archaeology: Why hunt for hours when you can just ask?
📈 Increasing in Value:
- Problem decomposition: Breaking vague requests into concrete, isolated steps.
- Codebase intuition: AI can search, but you know where to look.
- Context Curation: AI is only as good as the context you provide. The new skill is knowing exactly which files, interfaces, and constraints the model needs to solve the problem without hallucinating.
- Review Rigor & Security: AI suggests; you decide. You treat every AI output like a PR from a junior engineer — checking for logic errors, security vulnerabilities, and “code bloat” that looks working but is a nightmare to maintain.
- Systems thinking: Understanding how pieces connect in the broader architecture.
The developers who thrive aren’t the ones who type fastest. They’re the ones who think clearest.
AI as Thinking Partner: The Enterprise Stack
The biggest misconception about AI-assisted development is that it’s just a chatbot window. The reality is more nuanced, and at Gong, it’s architectural.
I have to give a massive shoutout to our DevEx team, who treat our internal developer platform as a first-class product. They keep us on the bleeding edge by treating our AI environment as a first-class platform. We run a containerized, secure instance of Claude Code, but the real power lies in our proprietary context mesh. By exposing our internal infrastructure via standard MCP interfaces, we’ve moved beyond ‘chatting with a bot’ to having an agent that can safely navigate our ecosystem.
Here is how that stack amplifies my efficiency:
- Claude Code (Containerized): It runs in a secure sandbox, allowing me to execute tests and refactor sensitive logic autonomously without risking data leakage.
- GitHub MCP: It helps me perform “software archaeology” by searching commit history and PR context to explain why a legacy line exists, saving hours of guessing.
- Codemaps MCP: Standard LLMs struggle with large codebases. We solved this with an internal tool leveraging GraphRAG. It generates a relational “brain map” of our repository (classes, DTOs, services), giving the AI instant architectural intuition so it doesn’t waste tokens hallucinating file paths and relations between entities.
- Figma MCP: It reads design tokens directly to scaffold components, ensuring the implementation matches the design system without manual transcription errors.
- Atlassian MCP: It connects my code changes back to the Jira requirements, ensuring I never miss an edge case defined in the product specs.
- Coralogix MCP: It queries production logs, traces, metrics for debugging.
The Debugging Flow: A Real Example
The report came from a power user: typing in our “Ask Me Anything” feature felt laggy for fast typists. The AMA component is a React/TypeScript textarea using our internal design system, Pitch — specifically its autoSize
prop.
First challenge: I couldn’t reproduce it. On my M1 Mac, everything felt smooth — even with Chrome DevTools CPU throttling. But I’ve learned to trust user feedback over my own hardware. So I turned to the stack.
Step 1: Measure, don’t guess
I asked Claude to help me add timing instrumentation to the component — performance.now()
markers around renders and keystroke handlers. After some rapid typing:
- Average: 5–7ms per keystroke
- Max spike: 44.90ms (nearly 3x the 16ms frame budget)
That spike was the smoking gun. Even if I couldn’t feel the lag, the numbers proved something was wrong.
Step 2: Form a hypothesis
I had a hunch about the autoSize
feature. I shared my suspicion with Claude: “I think it’s calling getComputedStyle on every keystroke, forcing synchronous layout recalculation.”
Claude didn’t just agree. It acted as a senior peer:
- Explained why
getComputedStyle
is expensive. In backend terms, it’s a blocking operation. It forces the browser to stop everything, recalculate the geometry of the entire page, and return the value before continuing. Doing this on every keystroke is like running a heavy SQL query inside afor
loop - Asked if I’d checked for multiple calls per keystroke.
- Pointed out the “layout thrashing” pattern.
I dug into the Pitch source code (using the GitHub MCP context) and found exactly that — calculateHeight()
was being called both in onChange
AND in a useEffect
watching value. Double execution on every keystroke.
Step 3: Document before fixing
Before writing any code, I created proper tickets using the Atlassian MCP:
— One for the user-facing issue (“AMA textarea typing is slow”)
— One for the root cause in the design system (“TextArea autoSize causes performance issues”)
Claude helped me structure clear descriptions with the technical findings — what I observed, what I measured, my hypothesis. The tickets became the single source of truth: status tracking, technical references, and later, the before/after performance metrics. When the fix shipped, anyone could trace the full story from report to resolution.
Step 4: Implement the fix
Claude and I discussed the approach: Batching the updates. Instead of recalculating on every keystroke, we used requestAnimationFrame
to ensure the expensive logic only runs once per frame (every ~16ms), dropping any intermediate calls. It’s essentially a high-speed debounce.
I implemented it, built the design system locally, linked it, and ran the same instrumentation:
- Max spike: 12–17ms (down from 44.90ms)
- ~60% improvement
Step 5: Code review as a learning loop
During review, a teammate flagged a subtle issue: “You should capture compRef.current
outside the RAF callback for closure safety.”
I could have just applied the fix and moved on. Instead, I asked Claude to explain the reasoning:
Me: Why should I capture compRef.current
outside the RAF callback?
Is it really risky?
Claude: It’s a defensive coding pattern. Here’s the scenario:
1.
calculateHeight()
is called
2. compRef.current exists, RAF is scheduled
3. Before RAF executes, something changes compRef.current
4. RAF callback runs with potentially stale referenceIt’s a classic concurrency issue. By the time the scheduled task runs, the world might have changed. Capturing the value upfront locks in the state, preventing a race condition where the function tries to read from a component that is already unmounting.
Your cleanup useEffect cancels the RAF on unmount, so it’s
unlikely to cause issues. But capturing the value upfront:const element = compRef.current;
Makes the code self-contained and easier to reason about.
It’s not fixing a critical bug — it’s making the code more robust.
I pushed the fix — but more importantly, I turned a review comment into a solidified mental model.
Step 6: Close the loop
After merging, I added the performance metrics as a comment on the Jira ticket (using atlassian MCP). Then I emailed the original reporter to verify the fix in production.
This is the shift: I didn’t spend my day console-logging or searching Stack Overflow for “textarea lag.” I spent it hypothesizing, measuring, and architectural reasoning.
The Efficiency Multiplier
Let’s talk numbers. These aren’t made up; they are from my actual week.
- Debug vague perf issue: Before AI: 1–2 days | With AI: 3 hours (4–6x)
- Learn new API/pattern: Before AI: 1–3 hours | With AI: 15 mins (4–12x)
- Write technical doc: Before AI: 1 hour | With AI: 15 mins (4x)
- Code review prep: Before AI: 20 mins | With AI: 5 mins (4x)
The multiplier isn’t constant — it depends on how well you can articulate what you need. A vague prompt gets a vague response. A precise question gets a precise answer.
What We Lose (And Why It’s Okay)
I’ll be honest: there are things I don’t memorize anymore. I don't remember the exact signature of every array method.
And I’m okay with that.
Because the tradeoff is: I solve harder problems. I ship faster. I spend my mental energy on architecture and user experience instead of syntax trivia.
I might not memorize the syntax, but I review the output more rigorously than ever. The developers who resist AI because “you should know this stuff” are optimizing for a game that’s already changed. Knowing syntax isn’t the point. Solving problems is the point.
The Gong Philosophy: Amplification
This mirrors exactly what we build for our customers. Just as Gong uses AI to help revenue teams focus on the conversation rather than taking notes, we use AI to focus on architecture rather than syntax.
It’s not about replacing the human; it’s about removing the drudgery so the human can provide value where it matters most.
The pyramid isn’t collapsing. It’s accelerating.
- Juniors ramp up faster because they have a patient, infinitely knowledgeable tutor available 24/7.
- Mid-level developers punch above their weight, tackling tasks that previously required senior oversight.
- Seniors become force multipliers — spending less time on implementation details and more time on design, mentorship, and “the hard stuff.”
The Human Parts That Remain
AI can’t do everything. Here’s what still requires a human:
- Data Integrity & Security: AI doesn’t know our compliance laws. A senior engineer knows what data is safe to share with an LLM and what must remain obfuscated. We are the guardians of customer trust.
- Taste: Knowing what should be built.
- Context: Understanding the business, the users, and the legacy code history.
- Accountability: Standing behind decisions when things break.
- Judgment calls: When the “right” answer depends on values, not logic.
The job isn’t “write code.” The job is “solve problems for humans, using code as one of many tools.” AI makes the code part easier. The human part is still on you.
The Bottom Line
Developers aren’t being replaced. We’re being upgraded.
The mundane parts are being automated. What remains is the interesting stuff: architecture, problem decomposition, judgment, creativity.
That’s not a downgrade. That’s the 10x Reality.
The question isn’t whether AI will change development. It already has. The question is whether you’ll use it to amplify your abilities — or watch from the sidelines while others do.
We’re building the future of Revenue AI, and we’re using the latest tools to do it. If you want to build the future of Revenue AI in the 10x Reality, check out our open engineering roles.
